segments.s193-g129: بسوی is the prepositional phrase 'toward' placed before its object. سپد کوه is an inverted adjective-noun phrase 'white mountain' (سپد is an archaic poetic adjective 'white' placed before the noun here). بنهاد is the classical past-tense verb (3rd person singular of بنهادن) meaning 'he set/placed' and here functions as 'he turned/turned his...'. روی is the direct object 'face' and follows the verb because of the poetic word order: literally 'toward the white mountain he set (his) face'.
segments.s193-g130: چنانچون is the comparative conjunction 'as/just as'. بود is the past copula 'was/were' placed before the subject in poetic inversion (so it reads 'as were ...'). مردم جنگجوی is a noun phrase 'the warlike people' with the modifier جنگجوی after مردم (typical Persian noun+adjective order). The whole segment forms a comparative clause modifying the previous action: 'as the warlike people were (doing)'.
segments.s193-g73: وزآنجا is the poetic fused sequence و + از آن‌جا meaning 'and from there/thereupon' and introduces narrative continuation. بیامد is a simple past/punctual verb 'came'. دلی پر ز غم is an NP: دلی (here used adjectivally/noun-like as 'a heart') + پرِ ز (پر 'full' governed by the preposition-like ز) + غم 'grief', literally 'a heart full of grief'. Word order is subjectless: the existential/experiential verb بیامد takes the following NP as its semantic subject.
segments.s193-g74: سری پر ز کینه is parallel to the previous NP: سری 'head' + پرِ ز کینه 'full of hatred'. بر here marks direction or motion toward and links to the verb گستهم (1st-person form) that follows; گستهم is an archaic/poetic first-person past/present-perfect 'I wandered / I was spread (about)' (here 'I wandered'). The clause lacks an explicit subject (typical of epic Persian) so the verb refers back to the narrator/speaker.
segments.s193-g75: کز is the relative/indefinite particle 'that/which/who' introducing a clause; here it functions like 'for/from which' in a causal/relative sense. اسپان is the plural of اسپ 'horses'. تو is the 2nd-person possessor 'your'. باره‌ای is a compound/poetic noun meaning 'a load / a bundle' (باره + ای), and دستکش is the item 'glove(s)'. Together they form 'a bundle/load of gloves from your horses'.
segments.s193-g76: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' marking the surface or locus of the striding; خرامد is a present/stative/epic-form verb 'to stride proudly, to swagger'. بافراز is an adverbial/poetic compound meaning 'with exaltation / high carriage' and خوش is an evaluative adverb/adjective 'pleasant/well/beautiful' modifying the manner of striding. Together the phrase asks 'where does he stride so proudly and splendidly?'.
segments.s193-g77: بده is the imperative 'give!'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that / in order that') and governs the subjunctive-like form بپوشم ('I may put on / I may don'); in Persian after تا + present-subjunctive one reads a purpose clause. سلیح نبرد is a genitive/attributive sequence: سلیح 'weapon' qualified by نبرد 'of battle' (i.e., 'war-weapon' or 'arm for battle').
segments.s193-g78: یکی here means 'someone / one (person)'. تا now functions temporally as 'until'. پدید آید is a light verb construction: پدید 'appear' + آید 'comes' giving 'comes into view / appears'. از introduces the source 'from', and مردمرد is an epic/composite adjective/noun meaning 'brave man(s)' (here 'a brave man'). The whole clause means 'until a brave man appears.'
segments.s193-g79: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing someone specific but unnamed). ترک is the noun 'Turk'. رفتست is an archaic/perfect past form 'has gone' (رفت + ـست). بر plus تیغ کوه is a locative phrase: بر 'on/upon' + تیغ 'point/edge (here: ridge)' + کوه 'mountain' — 'upon the mountain's ridge'.
segments.s193-g80: بدین سان is the fixed adverbial phrase 'in this way / thus' (here split into ب + دین + سان in the wordlist; taken together it means 'thus'). نظاره is a noun 'view / look / observation' used imperatively/optatively in context ('look!' / 'observe!'). برو is the imperative 'go!'. بر گروه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against the troop/band' (بر 'on/upon' + گروه 'troop, company'), giving a command-like sequence 'thus — observe, go against the troop.'
segments.s193-g9: همه یک ز دیگر دلاورترند: همه ('all/everyone') + یک ز دیگر ('one from another') expresses a reciprocal comparison: literally 'every one from another'. دلاورترند is the comparative adjective 'braver' with the 3rd-person plural copula ending -ند, so the clause means 'each is/brave than the next'. Word order (subject/quantifier + comparative predicate) is typical for classical Persian poetry.
segments.s193-g10: چو خورشید تابان بدو پیکرند: چو is the simile marker 'like/as'. خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective 'the shining sun'. بدو is the prepositional combination 'to it/unto it' (referring back to the sun or a previously mentioned referent). پیکرند is plural predicate 'they are figures/bodies' (پیکر + plural copula -ند). The clause thus compares the warriors to the shining sun, saying they are (in relation) its forms/figures.
segments.s193-g11: ولیکن خرد نیست با پهلوان: ولِیکن is the adversative conjunction 'but/yet'. خرد is the noun 'wisdom' and نیست is the negative copula 'is not'. با پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'with the hero' (possession/possession-like idiom meaning 'the hero does not possess wisdom'). The clause literally reads 'But wisdom is not with the hero.'
segments.s193-g12: سر بی‌خرد چون تن بی‌روان: this is a parallel simile. سر بی‌خرد = 'a head without sense' (noun + negative adjective بی‌خرد). چون introduces the simile 'like'. تن بی‌روان = 'a body without a soul/spirit' (تن + بی‌روان). The construction links two nominal phrases by comparison rather than using a finite verb.
segments.s193-g13: نباشند پیروز ترسم بکین: نباشند is an optative/negative form meaning 'there will not be' or 'may there not be' (classical negative of باشـدن for 3rd-person plural). پیروز is the predicate 'victorious' (used substantivally here: 'victors'). ترسم is a parenthetical first-person verb 'I fear' introducing the speaker's concern. بکین is a poetic/archaic particle/verb-form (here functioning adverbially or as an idiomatic tag tied to the situation); in context it does not act as a main lexical verb but colors the clause ('in this affair' / 'in this conflict').
segments.s193-g14: مگر خسرو آید بتوران زمین: مگر here means 'unless'. خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow'. آید is the present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to come' ('comes' / 'should come'). بتوران is the prepositional phrase 'to Turan' (be + place), and زمین here means 'land/country' — 'into the land of Turan.' The whole clause is the exception/condition to the previous fear.
segments.s193-g15: بکین پدر جمله پشت آوریم: بکین here is an archaic/poetic particle or imperative-like invocation (it appears in this poem as a fixed exclamatory/performative element). پدر may be vocative 'father' or a term addressing a leader/ancestor; جمله 'all' modifies the following phrase. پشت آوریم is a compound verb meaning 'we turn (our) backs' or 'we retreat', with آوریم in the 1st-person plural present/subjunctive form 'we bring/turn'. The sequence expresses a proposed collective action: 'let us all turn our backs.'
segments.s193-g16: مگر دشمنان را به مشت آوریم: مگر 'unless' introduces the exception. دشمنان را is the definite object 'the enemies' (را marks the direct object). به مشت آوریم is an idiom using به + مشت ('into/under the fist') plus the verb آوریم 'we bring' = 'we can seize/overpower' or 'bring (them) into our fists.' Grammatically this is a subordinate conditional: 'unless we can bring the enemies into our fists.'
segments.s193-g65: This is the reporting clause. بدو is the old-style enclitic-like pronoun meaning “to him/to that one” placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” نشنیدی is 2nd-person past negative (“didn't you hear?”) and از رهنمای is a prepositional phrase “from the guide” marking the source of the report.
segments.s193-g66: که introduces the subordinate content clause (“that …”). با رزمت is an instrumental/associative phrase “with your fighting / in battle,” اندیشه is the noun “thought/consideration” placed before the modal باید (‘must’) for emphasis, and بجای is an adverbial meaning roughly “appropriately / with regard to what is fitting.” The clause uses the modal باید to express necessity; the verb (infinitive or explicit action) is implied rather than stated.
segments.s193-g67: This is a coordinate negative: نه … نه … (“neither … nor …”). تو is the 2nd‑person subject, مغز داری is a simple present/past-tense construction meaning “you have wit/brains” (داری = ‘you have’), and رای و خرد are two nouns joined by و (‘and’) forming the second item of the correlative negation (“nor opinion/counsel and wisdom”).
segments.s193-g68: چنین گفت is the nominalized phrase “such a saying/utterance,” marked as a definite object by را. کس is an indefinite subject “someone/whosoever.” بکیفر برد is a fixed, somewhat archaic expression meaning “was punished / met with punishment” (here reported as a general consequence); برد is a past verb used idiomatically with بکیفر.
segments.s193-g69: دل بیژن is the subject “Bijan's heart.” آمد here is a copular/result verb “came” used idiomatically with a following state: ز تندی is an ablative phrase “from/through haste or impetuosity,” and بدرد is a verbal-adjectival phrase “to pain / into pain” indicating the resulting emotional state (i.e. his heart was pained by haste/anger).
segments.s193-g70: This clause contains the oath formula. بدادار دارنده is a compound epithet used as the noun (literary/poetic) being sworn by — literally something like “the just/possessing Lord” — سوگند is “an oath,” and خورد is the past verb in the fixed collocation سوگند خورد meaning “he swore.” In classical Persian سوگند خورد (“ate an oath”) is the idiom for taking an oath.
segments.s193-g71: که again introduces a subordinate clause of intention/purpose. زین را is the definite object “this saddle,” and نگردانم is a 1st-person singular negative jussive/subjunctive form (“I will not turn/remove/undo”) — here used in sense “I will not take off / remove.” از پشت اسپ is a locative/ablative phrase “from the horse's back” specifying the source from which the saddle would be removed.
segments.s193-g72: مگر introduces the exception/conditional “unless.” کشته آیم is a subjunctive/passive construction “I should be killed / I be slain,” where آیم is the subordinate present/subjunctive used with a passive participle. بِکین زرسپ (بکین + زرسپ) names the violent agency: بکین here carries the sense “vengeance / taking vengeance” and زرسپ is the person (Zarasp) associated with that vengeance, so the phrase means roughly “by the vengeance of Zarasp.”
segments.s193-g1: همی before a past verb creates an iterative or continuous sense (classical/poetic usage), so همی گفت here = ‘he kept saying’. جوشن (cuirass) is the direct object placed before the verb بست (past ‘bound/fastened’); گرم follows and functions adverbially/intensifying the action (‘firmly/ardently’). The clause literal order is: iterative adverb + verb + object + iterative adverb + verb + adverb.
segments.s193-g2: همی again marks a repeated or ongoing action. بر تنش is a prepositional phrase (‘on his body’), where -ش is the enclitic possessive ‘his’. The second بر appears before the verb بدرید as a particle meaning ‘upon/over’ and combines with بدرید (past ‘tore’) — poetic word order puts the verb before its nominal patient, so چرم (‘the leather’) appears after the verb.
segments.s193-g3: نشست is a past verb meaning ‘sat’ or ‘alighted’ (here ‘rose/descended from’); از بر is the compound prepositional idea ‘from off / from atop’. اژدهای is the noun ‘dragon’ followed by the adjectival/epithet دژم (‘grim’): classical Persian often places modifiers after the noun.
segments.s193-g4: خرامان is an adverb/gerund meaning ‘with a proud stride / striding proudly’ and precedes the past verb بیامد (‘came’). براه here marks motion ‘on/toward the road’ or ‘along the way’; the final noun چرم appears after it — in poetic diction the accompaniment or goal can follow the motion phrase, so the clause reads ‘striding proudly he came (on the road) (with/bringing) the leather’ (ellipsis of a linking preposition is common).
segments.s193-g5: فرود is a verbal noun (‘descent’ / ‘alighting’) used adverbially; placing سیاوش immediately after it identifies the agent (‘Siyâvash’). چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’, او را is the object with the definite/object marker را, and بدید is the past tense ‘saw’ — together: ‘When Siyâvash saw him (as he descended…)’. Classical Persian often uses nominalized actions (فرود) where modern speech would use a finite verb.
segments.s193-g6: یکی is an indefinite article-like usage ‘a’/‘one’. باد سرد is a noun phrase ‘a cold wind’; از + جگر is an ablative/genitive phrase ‘from the liver/heart’ (jigar used metaphorically as the seat of feeling). برکشید is a past transitive/intransitive ‘lifted up / rose up’, here intransitive with باد as subject: ‘a cold wind rose from his heart.’
segments.s193-g7: همی گفت again = ‘he kept saying’. کین here qualifies لشکر; in older Persian کین can mean a vengeful or hostile quality (poetically ‘this vengeful/hostile army’), and رزمساز is a compound epithet ‘battle‑making / war‑ready’ or ‘warrior’ modifying لشکر. The three words form a marked noun phrase placed after the reporting verb.
segments.s193-g8: ندانند is the negative present/plural form ‘they do not know’ (classical -ند ending for 3rd‑person plural). راه نشیب و فراز is a frozen collocation ‘the way/path of descent and ascent’ (i.e. how to handle downhill and uphill, or the ups and downs of a route). Together: ‘they do not know the way of descent and ascent.’
segments.s193-g33: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بر زه is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the string' (بر = on; زه = bowstring). بشست is a past-form verb here meaning 'he drew/fastened/settled' (Ferdowsi uses terse/archaic verb forms; functionally a past action tied to چو). اندر آری گره groups as a following adverbial/nominal phrase: اندر = 'within/in', آری here functions as an emphatic/qualifying element ('indeed/therein' in older usage), and گره = 'knot'—together 'set/placed a knot inside.' The whole segment introduces a temporal/causal circumstance for the result in the next clause.
segments.s193-g34: خدنگت is 'your dagger' (خدنگ + enclitic -ت). نیابد is the present/subjunctive form of یابیدن with negative prefix ن- here rendered 'does not/ cannot find' (Ferdowsi often uses this form to express inability). گذر بر زره is the object: گذر = 'passage/way' and بر زره = 'through/on the armor' (بر = over/against/through, زره = armor). Together: 'your dagger cannot find a passage through the armor.'
segments.s193-g35: سلیح سیاوش is a possessive/nominal phrase with the possessed noun first (classical ordering): 'the arms/weapons of Siyâvash.' بپوشد is third-person (present/subjunctive narrative) of پوشیدن 'to put on' meaning 'he dons/puts on.' بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival phrase 'for battle' or 'to battle' (بِ + جنگ = 'into/for war').
segments.s193-g36: نترسد is the negative present/indefinite of ترسیدن with the negative prefix ن-: 'he does not fear' (used as a general statement). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. The sequence پیکان تیر خدنگ lists weapons as causes of fear ('arrows, shafts, daggers'); classical Persian often strings nouns without ezâfe for enumeration. The whole segment means 'he fears not arrows, shafts, nor daggers.'
segments.s193-g37: بکش here is a verbal root meaning 'pull/draw' (imperative form is possible, but in narrative it functions as a simple/action verb 'he drew/sent'); چرخ is used concretely as 'wheel' (poetic martial image) and together with پیکان 'arrows' forms the direct objects. سوی اسپ ران is a prepositional/ directional phrase: سوی = 'toward', اسپ = 'steed/horse', ران = 'hindquarter/flank' (target of the missiles). Grammatically the verb governs the coordinated objects and the directional phrase.
segments.s193-g38: مگر introduces an exception 'unless'. خسته گردد is a subjunctive/potential construction: خسته = 'tired/weary' and گردد (from گشتن/شدن) = 'becomes' or 'may become'—together 'becomes weary.' هیون گران is a noun phrase with adjective after noun (classical order): هیون = 'beast/animal', گران = 'heavy' (here 'stout/heavy beast'). So: 'unless the heavy beast becomes weary.'
segments.s193-g39: پیاده شود is a verbal phrase 'to become on foot / to dismount' (پیاده = on foot; شود = becomes/shall), used here as a potential/result clause. بازگردد is the verb 'return' (present/subjunctive/narrative third-person). مگر again introduces an exception 'unless.' The trio thus states the expected action '(he) will dismount and return, unless...' with classical word order.
segments.s193-g40: کشان is a participial/agent-like form 'dragging' or 'being dragged' that modifies the following image. چون = 'like/as', سپهبد = 'spahbed' (a high-ranking general), and بگردن سپر = a prepositional-like phrase 'with a shield around the neck' (بگردن = around the neck; سپر = shield). The segment paints the exception: 'unless he is dragged off like a spahbed with a shield round his neck.'
segments.s193-g41: کمان را is the direct object with the postpositive object marker را (object marker after the noun). بزه کرد is a compound: کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made' and بزه here is a nominal element combined with کرد to mean ‘drew/shot (the bow)’ or otherwise describes the action done to the bow (the exact lexical force of بزه is archaic/poetic). جنگی modifies the following action as 'warlike'; فرود (here postpositive) means 'downward' or 'sent down' and functions adverbially to qualify the action. Word order is Subject (implied) – Object – Verb – adverbial modifier, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s193-g42: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/after that'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. قبضه is 'grip/handle' and چرخ here is a noun joined to قبضه (literally 'the wheel/round part's grip'); بر کف is a prepositional phrase 'upon the palm' (بر + کف). بسود is a prepositional/adiunct phrase meaning 'to the advantage / for profit', completing the clause. The clause follows the typical order adverb – demonstrative – noun phrase – prepositional phrase – resultative phrase.
segments.s193-g43: بزد is the past tense verb 'he struck/shot' (simple past). تیر is the direct object 'arrow' (no را marker but interpreted as object), and بر introduces the target 'into/on'. سینه اسپ is a genitival sequence 'the steed's chest' (sineh + noun), with گیو following as the possessor (Giv): standard Persian possessor ordering here is possessee then possessor. The clause order is Verb – Object – prepositional target – possessor phrase, a common poetic ordering where the verb appears before or adjacent to its object.
segments.s193-g44: فرود آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'fell down / came down' (فرود = down/descent + آمد = came) and expresses the result of the preceding hit. از introduces the cause or source 'from / because of', باره here denotes the impact/instance or 'blow/occasion' causing the effect, and برگشت is a past/singular noun/verb form 'returned / was turned back' (in context it denotes reversal or recoiling). نیو is a short poetic tag at the line's end (its lexical sense here is uncertain/poetic). The clause is a sequence showing result (fell) then cause/repercussion introduced by از.
segments.s193-g45: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (short form of از), followed by بام 'roof' as the place. سپد کوه is a compound adjective+noun where سپد (an archaic/poetic word meaning 'white' or 'bright') qualifies کوه 'mountain' — 'the white mountain'. خنده is the subject or event 'laughter', and بخاست is the past tense verb 'arose/rose up'. Word order here is prepositional phrase of place/time – subject – verb, typical in poetry to front the locative.
segments.s193-g46: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning roughly 'still/then/indeed' and introduces the following clause. مغز is 'brain' or 'mind' and here stands for intellect or spirit; گیو is the possessive proper name 'Giv'. از marks source/reason 'by/from', گواژه is 'witness/testimony', and بکاست is an archaic past form meaning 'was diminished / decreased' (the lexical entries given are not ideal, but syntactically بکاست functions as the main past verb). The clause thus states an effect on Giv's mind caused by the testimony; subject and possessor order follows classical Persian patterns.
segments.s193-g47: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/advanced' (verb agrees with plural subject). گردان همه is the noun phrase 'all the troops/squadrons' with همه 'all' following گردان (word order noun + quantifier/emphasis). پیش گیو is a prepositional/locative phrase 'before/in front of Giv' (پیش + proper name). The clause therefore means 'all the troops advanced before Giv.'
segments.s193-g48: که here introduces the content of their utterance ('that/that they said'): یزدان is 'God' and سپاس‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory phrase meaning 'thanks/praise' (the intervening ای marks an address or exclamation). سپهدار is the title 'commander' (vocative or appositive), and نیو is a short poetic name or tag at the line end; together the phrase is an address or acclamation: 'God be thanked, O commander Niyu.' Grammatically this is a reported utterance introduced by که.
segments.s193-g49: که introduces a proposition ('that'); اسپ است خسته is a simple nominal clause 'the horse is tired' (اسپ = archaic/epic form of اسب). تو خسته نه‌ای is a second clause addressing 'you' with خسته (tired) and the copula نه‌ای (classical negative/existential form) meaning 'you are not (tired)'; the two clauses are conjoined without an explicit conjunction in epic style.
segments.s193-g50: توان شد is a composite meaning roughly 'it became possible' or 'it could (be done)'; دگر بار = 'again'; بار is 'occasion/time'; بسته نه‌ای uses the past participle بسته ('tied/fastened') with the copula نه‌ای to give the negative 'is not/was not tied'. Together the phrase means that (it) could not be tied again.
segments.s193-g51: برگیو ('بر' + 'گیو') is a prepositional phrase 'upon Giv' placed before the verb; شد functions as a verbal particle meaning 'it happened / he came' in epic narration. بیژن شیر مرد is an appositive noun phrase: 'Bijan, a lion-man' (شیر مرد = 'lion of a man' = valiant man) used as an epithet.
segments.s193-g52: فراوان (adverb/adjective) modifies the following plural object سخنها ('many words'); the verb بگفت is past 'he said/spoke'. از introduces the topic 'regarding/about' and نبرد names the subject matter 'the battle'. Word order here is typical Persian: modifier + object + verb, with از marking the theme.
segments.s193-g53: که‌ای here is a vocative/exclamatory particle ('O' / 'that O' in epic diction) addressing someone; باب looks to be a title or name element attached to the following proper names (شیراوژن تیزچنگ), which are two epithets/names in apposition. This whole segment is a vocative phrase calling the addressee.
segments.s193-g54: کجا here functions as a rhetorical 'why'/'where' opening the question; پیل = 'elephant'; با + تو literally 'with you' or 'against (in connection to) you' depending on context; نرفتی is the 2nd-person singular past negative 'you did not go' and بجنگ is the infinitive 'to fight'. Together: 'Why did you not go to fight the elephant?'
segments.s193-g55: چرا is the question word 'why'; دید is past 'he saw'; پشت ترا is the object 'behind you' with the 2nd-person clitic ت on پشت and the object marker را (here written separately) marking it as definite; یک سوار 'a rider' completes the object phrase. Word order places the questioned element at the start and the verb after it, typical in narrative questions.
segments.s193-g56: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'); دست تو بودی is literally 'was your hand' — an idiom meaning 'was at your side / your aide' with بودی the 2nd-person past 'you were' (here agrees with the rider: 'who was your hand'). بهر کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'for the battle' (بهر = 'for').
segments.s193-g25: 'بسی' is an emphatic/adverbial 'many' placed before the verb phrase; 'بی‌پدر' is an adjective 'fatherless' describing the object; 'کرد' is the past transitive verb 'made/caused' (here causative: 'made fatherless'); the object 'فرزند خرد' ('child small') is a noun phrase with the modifier after the head (noun + adjective) meaning 'a young/small child'.
segments.s193-g26: Again 'بسی' qualifies the following list; the coordinated nouns 'کوه و رود و بیابان' ('mountain and river and wilderness') are the objects or destinations of the final verb; 'سپرد' (past of سپردن) is transitive 'entrusted/consigned/left' — the subject is omitted and carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s193-g27: 'پدر' is the subject and 'نیز' 'also' adds him to the previous doer; 'ازو' ('from him' / 'on his account') gives the cause; 'شد' is the past of شدن 'became'; 'بسی بی‌پسر' is a predicative phrase meaning 'greatly sonless' or 'bereft of many sons' (بسی intensifies the adjective).
segments.s193-g28: 'بپی' is an archaic/obscure particle here functioning adverbially (roughly 'again' / 'thereupon'); 'بسپرد' (alternative spelling of سپرد) is the past transitive 'he handed over/placed'; the noun phrase 'گردن شیر نر' ('neck of male-lion') is the direct object, i.e. 'he put his neck before/into the male lion' (submitted himself to the lion).
segments.s193-g29: 'بایران' stands first as a locative/adverbial (here likely a place or place-name); 'برادرت را' is the definite object marked by 'را' (برادر + possessive suffix -ت = 'your brother'); 'او' is the subject pronoun 'he'; 'کشید' is the past of کشیدن used transitively here — contextually 'slew/struck down' (an archaic epic use of کشیدن).
segments.s193-g30: 'بجیحون' is an adverbial phrase 'to/at the Jeyhun' (the Oxus river); 'گذر کرد' is a compound past ('made passage' = 'crossed'); 'و' links the clauses; 'کشتی ندید' places the object 'کشتی' before the negative-past verb 'ندید' ('he did not see a ship').
segments.s193-g31: 'وراگیو' is a proper/name or epithet; 'خوانند' is the present plural 'they call (him)'; 'پیلست' is another epithet/name attached after the verb; the phrase ends with 'و بس' ('and enough' / 'and that is all'), a closing formula meaning 'no more need be said'.
segments.s193-g32: 'که' introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'); 'در رزم' is the locative 'in battle'; 'دریای نیلست' is a verbless/adjectival clause where 'نیلست' means 'is Nile-blue' (نیل + است fused), describing the sea; final 'و بس' again closes with 'and enough/that suffices.'
segments.s193-g121: This clause contains an implicit possessive construction and a copular verb: 'دل گیو شد' = 'Giv's heart became...'. Classical Persian often places the possessee before the possessor without an explicit ezafe (here 'دل' + 'گیو'), followed by the past copula 'شد' (became). The remainder 'زان سخن پر ز دود' is a causal/qualifying phrase: 'زان' (from/for that) introduces 'سخن' (speech), then 'پر' (full) governs the 編itive 'ز دود' (of smoke) in inverted poetic order (predicate 'پر' before the 'ز' phrase).
segments.s193-g122: 'چو اندیشه کرد' is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by 'چو' = 'when'; 'اندیشه' (thought) combines with the light verb 'کرد' (did/made) to mean 'he thought/pondered'. The complement 'از گشاد فرود' uses 'از' in the sense 'about/concerning' and a postnominal adjective 'گشاد' (wide/open) modifying 'فرود' (descent), in the compact, poetic word order.
segments.s193-g123: Two actions are linked by the coordinating 'و' (and): 'فرستاد' (he sent) and 'بخواند' (he caused to be read/called). 'مر' is the beneficiary/indirect object 'to me', placed before the person summoned; 'گستهم را' is the definite object marked by 'را' (here the person summoned). In this literary register 'بخواند' can mean 'had someone recite' or 'summoned' depending on context.
segments.s193-g124: 'بسی' is an adverbial quantifier ('many/very') modifying the noun phrase 'داستانهای نیکو' (fine stories), where the plural noun 'داستانهای' is followed by its adjective 'نیکو'. The verb 'براند' is a poetic/present-tense form meaning 'brought forth' or 'related' (here 'he set forth/told'), placed after the noun phrase as is common in epic diction.
segments.s193-g125: The verb 'فرستاد' (he sent) governs the following noun phrase. 'درع سیاوش' is a possessive-like juxtaposition meaning 'Siavash's armor' (the ezafe is omitted in epic style). The final item 'برش' appears to mark the recipient/destination or refer back to 'to him' as an enclitic/old form; its exact function here is archaic or dialectal and is not rendered by the senses supplied.
segments.s193-g126: 'همان خسروانی' = 'that/that same royal (one)' with 'خسروانی' qualifying 'همان'. 'یکی' is an indefinite numeral/article 'one/a certain', and 'مغفرش' is the noun 'مغفر' (a covering/helmet) plus the enclitic '-ش' = 'his', so the phrase means 'one (helmet) of his, a royal one.' The compact ordering and enclitic possession are typical of the epic register.
segments.s193-g127: The verb 'بیاورد' (he brought) appears before 'گستهم' in the verb‑first poetic order, so 'گستهم' functions as the actor (a proper name). 'درع نبرد' is a noun modified by 'نبرد' (battle) used adjectivally to mean 'battle-armor' or 'war-shield'.
segments.s193-g128: 'بپوشید' (he put on/was wearing) is a past verb and is followed by the subject 'بیژن' in the inverted poetic order. The phrase 'بکردار گرد' is a predicative cluster: 'بکردار' (of good/virtuous deeds/character) modified by the archaic/poetic verb-form 'گرد', which functions like 'became/turned' (an older alternative to 'گشت'/'گردید'), so together they mean 'became of good conduct' or 'was made noble in deed'.
segments.s193-g81: چنین داد پاسخ که is a fixed formula meaning “he answered thus” (چنین = thus; داد پاسخ = gave an answer). این نیست روی follows as the content of that reply: این (this/the present matter) + نیست (is not) + روی (literally “face/appearance” here: ‘an occasion/thing to put one’s face to’). The clause states a negated copula ('this is not ...') and leads into the following advisory clauses.
segments.s193-g82: ابر خیره گرد بلاها مپوی is a sequence of imperatives/advisory negatives continuing the quoted reply. ابر (cloud) + خیره (staring/astonished) + گرد here represents verb/verb-like element from گردیدن (‘turn/become’) or part of the verbal phrase, producing a sense like ‘do not stand there staring like a cloud’; بلاها (calamities) is the object/topic and مپوی is negative imperative from پوییدن (‘do not seek/pursue’). Overall: ‘do not gaze at the clouds; do not go after calamities.’
segments.s193-g83: زرسپ سپهدار names a person with title: زرسپ (proper name) + سپهدار (title/commander). چون here is the comparative/linking particle 'like/as' and relates زرسپ سپهدار to the following name ریونیز (another person). The phrase is elliptical: 'Zarasp the sepahdar, like Rioniz ...'.
segments.s193-g84: سپهبد is another title (spahbed). که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'), and گیتی ندارد بچیز literally orders as 'in the world (گیتی) has (ندارد) nothing/anything (بچیز)'. ندارد is the present/past-stative of داشتن negated here ('has not'); بچیز functions as 'anything/nothing' (i.e. 'has nothing'). The clause modifies the spahbed.
segments.s193-g85: پدرت is 'your father' (پدر + 2nd-person suffix -ت). آنکه is the relative 'he who'. پیل ژیان is a compound noun: پیل 'elephant' + ژیان 'living/life', giving 'living elephant(s)'; بشکرد is an archaic past form meaning 'broke/smash(ed)'. The whole segment = 'your father, he who smashed living elephants'.
segments.s193-g86: بگردنده is a participial noun 'the wanderer/roamer'. گردون (گردون) is the poetic word for 'the revolving sky/heavens'. همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'still'/'indeed' and precedes the verb ننگرد, here negative/indicative of نگریدن ('does not look/regard'). Together: 'the revolving heavens still do not look on the wanderer.'
segments.s193-g87: ازو (from him/of him) begins the clause; بازگشتند is the past plural 'they returned/turned back'. دل پر ز درد is a noun phrase 'hearts full (پر) of pain (ز درد)' — ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and درد is emotional/physical 'pain/sorrow'. The whole: 'They returned from him, hearts full of pain.'
segments.s193-g88: کس here functions negatively as 'no one' (not 'someone'); آورد is past 'brought' (used idiomatically: 'accomplished' or 'came forth to do it'). با + کوه خارا is 'with the flinty/hard mountain' (خارا modifies کوه). نکرد is the past negative 'did not do' — the sequence expresses that no one succeeded or no one could do anything against the hard rock/mountain (i.e. 'no one could move/overcome the flinty mountain').
segments.s193-g97: بدو گفت پس گستهم راه نیست — Literally 'he said to him: then گستهم راه is not'. بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (verb of speaking attaches to it); گفت is the simple past 'said'; پس here means 'then/so'; گستهم is an archaic/poetic form referring to the speaker’s state (literally 'I am spread/wide' or 'I have room/capacity') functioning as the topic; راه نیست is the negated verbless clause 'there is no way' (نیست = 'is not'). The whole segment expresses a refusal or inability: 'he said to him that, then, there is no way for me.'
segments.s193-g98: خرد خود از این تیزی آگاه نیست — خرد خود = 'my/one's own reason' (خرد = wisdom/reason; خود = reflexive/possessive 'own'); از این تیزی = 'of/about this tizi' where از + این marks the domain ('concerning this...') and تیزی in this context means 'swiftness/keenness' (here: speed); آگاه نیست is the predicative phrase 'is not aware' (آگاه = aware; نیست = is not). Together: 'my reason is not aware of this swiftness.'
segments.s193-g99: جهان پرفراز و نشیبست و دشت — جهان 'the world'; پرفراز و نشیبست is a fixed poetic description 'highs and lows / full of ups and downs' (پرفراز = lofty/high; نشیبست = low/descending), with the copula/nominal suffix -ست marking state; و دشت adds 'and (there is) a plain' (دشت = plain). This is a general observation about the landscape/life.
segments.s193-g100: گر ایدونک زینجا بباید گذشت — Conditional clause introduced by گر 'if'; ایدونک is a small poetic particle meaning roughly 'thus/so/a little like this' (literary); زینجا 'from here' (ز + اینجا = from here); بباید is the present/necessitative form 'one must / it is necessary' (ب- prefix + بایَ/باید); گذشت is the infinitive/past-stem 'to pass' or noun 'passage' used here as the verbal idea 'to pass by'. Together: 'if one must pass from here even in this way...' (leaving the condition open).
segments.s193-g101: مرا بارگیر اینک جوشن کشد — مرا بارگیر is an imperative-like construction meaning 'take my load' or 'bear me/load me' (مرا = me/my as direct object; بارگیر = lit. 'load-bearer' or 'take the load' functioning as a command/request). اینک 'now' (poetic 'now/behold'); جوشن 'the cuirass/armor'; کشد is a present/subjunctive/optative-form of کشیدن 'to pull/drag' (here 'is tugging' or 'will tug'). The clause says: 'Take my burden; now the cuirass tugs.'
segments.s193-g102: دو ماندست اگر زین یکی را کشد — دو ماندست = 'two remain' (ماندست = remains/is left); اگر 'if'; زین 'of/from these saddles' (زین = saddle/harness); یکی را 'one (of them)' with را marking the definite object/topic; کشد again 'pulls/drag(s)'. The conditional: 'Two will remain if one of these saddles pulls.'
segments.s193-g103: نیابم دگر نیز همتای او — نیابم is a negative/archaic verb form meaning 'I do not find / I cannot find' (نـ- negative prefix + یابم subjunctive/present); دگر نیز 'another also / no other' (دگر = other; نیز = also/emphatic); همتای او = 'his equal / counterpart' (همتا = equal; او = him). Together: 'I cannot find another who equals him.'
segments.s193-g104: برنگ و تگ و زور و بالای اوی — A series of coordinated qualities introduced without a verb: برنگ 'in color' (on color/hue); و تگ 'and tæg' (tæg here a short poetic term for swiftness/quickness or lit. narrow/single; context = agility/speed); و زور 'and strength/power'; و بالای اوی 'and his superiority/loftiness' (بالای = superiority/height; اوی = his/her — possessive clitic in old/poetic Persian). The list modifies همتای او: 'equal to him in color, speed, strength, and loftiness.'
segments.s193-g17: بگوکین is an archaic/poetic imperative-like formula (بگو + کین) meaning “say / tell (of) this.” The nominal phrase سوار سرافراز is the thing to be identified, and the clause ends with the copula کیست (“who is”), so the whole segment asks “say who this lofty rider is.”
segments.s193-g18: که introduces a relative/casual clause (“for/that”), linking to the rider. بر دست و تیغش literally “at/on his hand and (at) his sword” (دست can be literal or figurative “power”), and بباید + گریست is an impersonal/modal construction meaning “one must/it is necessary to weep.” The verb گریست here is the action required (“to weep/lament”).
segments.s193-g19: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase “he looked/gazed.” ز + افراز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase “from the lofty height/eminence.” تخوار is the object of the gaze (here “the wretch/lowly person”) placed after the prepositional phrase in classical word order.
segments.s193-g20: ببی دانشی here reads as a title/name or epithet (ببی = lady/matron, دانشی = ‘of knowledge’ / learned), followed by the locative phrase بر چمن “on the meadow.” رست is a past tense “grew” and خار is the thing that grew (“a thorn”); the verb and subject appear in the classical order where the verb precedes the noun for poetic effect.
segments.s193-g21: بدو is the dative pronoun “to him/unto him” and گفت is the past tense ‘he said’ (classical phrasing: ‘he said to him’). کین functions as an epithet (here conveying vengeance/hatred), modifying اژدهای دژم (‘the grim/fiendish dragon’), so the phrase names the feared creature being spoken about.
segments.s193-g22: که introduces a relative clause describing that dragon. مرغ از هوا اندر آرد is a classical word order meaning “brings/takes a bird from the air” (اندر آرد = brings down), and the final بدم is a possessive/nominal element (“my blood” or figuratively “my ruin/undoing”), indicating the speaker’s loss or danger associated with the dragon’s action.
segments.s193-g23: که again links causally/explanatorily. دست نیای تو is a genitive phrase “the hand/power of your forefather” (نیای تو = your ancestor). پیران is the object ‘the elders’ and ببست is the past-tense verb “bound/tied/closed,” so the clause says that your ancestor’s power bound the elders.
segments.s193-g24: دو لشکر is “two armies.” ز ترکان indicates source/identity “from/of the Turks,” and بهم برشکست is a compound verb phrase meaning “were shattered/scattered” (بهم here emphasizes collective/scattered disorder, برشکست = broke/shattered). Together: two Turkish armies were broken.
segments.s193-g105: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial 'to him' (literally 'unto him') placed before the verb; گفت is the past 3rd-person verb 'said'; بیژن is the speaker's name (subject) placed after the verb in poetic word order; بکین is a poetic form of بکن ('do' / 'take/act') used here as an imperative or hortative; زرسپ is a proper name (vocative or object of the imperative). The whole segment marks reported speech: 'He said to him: …'.
segments.s193-g106: پیاده is an adverbial phrase 'on foot'; بپویم is first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I will go / I go' (poetic verb form); نخواهم is a negative of خواستن ('I do not want / I will not take'), here denying desire to use the horse; خود is an emphatic/reflexive 'itself / myself / own' modifying اسپ; اسپ is the noun 'horse / steed' and is the object of نخواهم. In sequence, the speaker says he will go on foot and refuses the horse for himself.
segments.s193-g107: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner of the reply; داد is the past verb 'gave' used with پاسخ to form 'gave an answer' (i.e. 'replied'); پاسخ is the object 'answer / reply'; بدو 'to him' marks the addressee; گستهم is a poetic/verbal form indicating the speaker's act of speaking or replying (first person in context). Together this frames the reported answer that follows.
segments.s193-g108: که introduces the reported content (that…); مویی literally 'a hair' used idiomatically as 'even a single hair / the slightest bit'; نخواهم is present/volitional negative 'I will not / I do not wish' (here 'I would not take'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (short form of 'از'); تو 'you' is the source; بیش و کم 'more and less' is a fixed binomial meaning 'more or less / any more or less amount' — together: 'I would not take one hair from you, neither more nor less.'
segments.s193-g109: مرا‌گر is a poetic conditional phrase 'if (it were) to me / if I had'; بود is the past/conditional copula 'were / was' used in the protasis (if‑clause); بارگی is a noun 'leaf / foliage' (here a unit counted); ده هزار follows as the numeral 'ten thousand' modifying بارگی. Poetic inversion puts the conditional word before the verb and the counted noun after the verb.
segments.s193-g110: همه دم 'every tip/edge' (here ‘each margin’ of a leaf) — دم in poetic usage can mean 'edge' or 'tip'; پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of'; گوهر is the noun 'gems / jewels'; شاهوار is an adjective 'regal / kingly' modifying گوهر. The phrase describes the imagined leaves being entirely studded with royal jewels.
segments.s193-g111: ندارم is the present negative 'I do not have / I would not have' used idiomatically to mean 'I would not be reluctant'; بدین here functions as 'to give this' / 'to give that' (poetic compound of به+این) marking what would be given; از تو 'from you' (marks the beneficiary/source in this idiom); آن را 'that' with را as the definite object marker; دریغ is a noun 'reluctance / reluctance to withhold' — the whole clause expresses 'I would have no reluctance to give that to you.' Note: بدین is a poetic fused form; parse and sense must be read as a single idiom meaning 'to give/withhold this'.
segments.s193-g112: نه ... و نه ... is the standard correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'; the enumerated nouns گنج (treasure), جان (life), اسپ (horse/steed), تیغ (sword) are objects that the speaker says he would not withhold — i.e. 'neither treasure nor life nor horse nor sword.' Word order lists the items in parallel after the correlative negative.
segments.s193-g57: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ترکی here is a noun 'the Turk' (not an adjective). چنین is an attributive 'such/so' modifying the following noun. اسپ خسته is a normal noun + adjective sequence ('steed, tired'); Persian allows the adjective to follow the noun. بدست is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'into the hand / into the possession' indicating the steed came into someone's control.
segments.s193-g58: برفتی is an archaic past-form verb meaning 'departed/went off' (Ferdowsi uses a -ی ending in past narrative). سراسیمه is an adverb 'in a fluster / frantically'. برسان here is a verb 'to bring / to deliver' used with an intended object. مست is a modifier/noun 'the drunk' (i.e. the intoxicated person) — together this gives 'he hurried off, flustered, to bring (it) to the drunk.'
segments.s193-g59: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb. گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses are nominal; the verb sense is the intended meaning). چون introduces a temporal/causal clause ('when/after' or 'since'). کشته شد is the periphrastic passive 'was killed' (کشته + شد, with شد as auxiliary). بارگی in this line is best read as a proper name or specific referent (not the literal 'leaf' sense) and is the patient referenced in the clause.
segments.s193-g60: بدو again marks the indirect object 'to him'. دادمی is an archaic past form 'gave' (داد + -می/-ی ending used in epic Persian). سر is the direct object 'head' (literal, i.e. a decapitated head). به یکبارگی is a prepositional phrase 'at once / suddenly' that modifies the giving action. Word order here is somewhat flexible/poetic: indirect object before verb, then the direct object and adverbial.
segments.s193-g61: همی is the iterative/continuous marker used with past narrative to mean 'kept on / kept on doing'. گفت here is again the past verb 'said' (the listed lemma senses are nominal; the verb sense is intended). گفتارهای درشت is the object phrase 'coarse / rude speeches' (گفتارهای is the plural 'utterances/speeches' and درشت 'coarse, rude' modifies it).
segments.s193-g62: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بیژن is the proper name 'Bijan' as the subject of the following verb. چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'so/thus' referring back to the situation. دید is the past verb 'saw / beheld'. بنمود is a past verb 'showed / exhibited' and پشت is the direct object 'back' (i.e. 'he showed/turned his back'). The clause reads 'when Bijan saw this, he showed his back.'
segments.s193-g63: برآشفت is a past-tense verbal compound 'became angry / was enraged'. گیو is the proper name 'Giv' and is the subject. از is the preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause. گشاد برش here forms a noun phrase describing what angered him (literally words like 'wide' or 'loose' — i.e. boastful/loose talk); the precise lexical senses in the supplied list do not cleanly capture this idiomatic compound, so the phrase functions as 'because of his loose/boastful speech.'
segments.s193-g64: یکی here functions adverbially 'once / one time' or 'a certain (strike)'. تازیانه is the instrument 'a whip'. بزد is the past verb 'he struck / smote'. بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'on his head' with the enclitic ش = 'his'. The clause therefore means 'he struck him once on the head with a whip.'
segments.s193-g89: مگر functions here as the conditional 'unless'. The clause پر کرگس بود رهنمای is a copular clause (NP + بود + predicate): 'پر کرگس' = 'the vulture's feather' is the subject/NP, بود is the past copula 'was', and رهنمای is the predicate 'guide'. Together: 'unless the vulture's feather was (his) guide.'
segments.s193-g90: وگرنه = 'otherwise/otherwise (then)'. بران دژ = 'toward that fortress' (بران = 'on/at that, toward that'); که introduces a relative clause modifying دژ. پوید is a present tense poetic form 'hastens/presses on', and بپای (here from پاییدن) means 'to lie in wait/ambush' or 'be in ambush'; together the relative clause says 'which lies in ambush/awaits', so the whole segment means 'otherwise (he) would hurry to that fortress which lies in ambush.'
segments.s193-g91: بدو گفت بیژن is 'Bijan said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' — narrative past). که introduces the quoted/request clause. مشکن is the negative imperative 'do not break/spoil', and دل + م is the older split form of the possessive 'دلَم' = 'my heart' (م is the 1st‑person possessive suffix written separately here). So: 'Bijan said to him, "Do not break my heart."'
segments.s193-g92: کنون is the adverb 'now' modifying the following verb. یال و بازو = 'the mane and (the) arm' (coordinate noun phrase). ز هم (poetic order for از هم) = 'from each other' / 'apart'. بگسلم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative of گسَل/گسستن with the prefix بـ and suffix ـم: 'let me rend/let me tear apart' or 'I will tear apart'. Together: 'now let me tear the mane and the arm apart (from each other).' Note the verb carries the speaker's volition (1st person).
segments.s193-g93: یکی here functions as an introductory 'one/once' (a certain time); سخت سوگند = 'a strong/solemn oath'. خوردم is the past 1st‑person form used idiomatically with سوگند ('سوگند خوردم' = 'I swore/took an oath'). بماه = 'by the moon' (the usual poetic oath form 'به ماه'). The whole segment: 'I once took a solemn oath by the moon.'
segments.s193-g94: This is a noun phrase in apposition/extension to the oath: بدادار here is an epithet/title (used poetically of a divine/sovereign power), گیهان = 'the world' (so 'بدادار گیهان' = 'the (just/sovereign) lord of the world'), و links to دیهیم شاه = 'the rural/country king' (an epithet meaning 'king of the district/land'). Grammatically these nouns complete the oath's object ('I swore by ...').
segments.s193-g95: کزین = 'from this' (poetic fused preposition + demonstrative). ترک here is the noun 'Turk' (the captor); من is the subject 'I' (emphatic placed after ترک); برنگردانم is the negative present/future 1st‑person form of برگرداندن/'to return/restore' meaning 'I will not get back/restore' (in context: 'I will not get back'), and اسپ = 'steed/horse' is the object. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: 'From this Turk my horse I will not get back.'
segments.s193-g96: زمانم = 'my time' / 'my fate' with the possessive suffix -م. سراید is a present/optative poetic form of سرودن/سراییدن meaning 'sings/comes to pass' (here 'will come to pass' or 'will be fulfilled'). مگر = 'unless/except', چون = 'like/as', and زرسپ is a proper name (Zarrasp). The clause says roughly 'my time will be spent/come to pass unless (it is) like Zarrasp' — i.e., a conditional exception introduced by مگر with a simile introduced by چون.
segments.s193-g113: This is an imperative clause: برو (go) is the 2nd‑person imperative telling someone to depart or move. یک بیک is an epithet-like phrase placed before the noun (here addressing or enumerating items) and بارگیها is the plural noun (the things to be inspected). ببین is a second imperative 'see/look' that completes the command. Word order is Verb — (modifier) — Object — Verb (imperative sequence), a common poetic/orderly phrasing.
segments.s193-g114: کدامت (which) is the interrogative/pronominal adjective with enclitic -ت indicating 'to you' or 'of you', followed by the verbal phrase به آید (comes/suits) where به is a preposition 'to/for' and آید is the present/subjunctive/optative of آمدن 'to come' used here idiomatically 'to befit/suit'. یکی (one) is the item to be selected and برگزین is the singular imperative 'choose!' that closes the sentence. The clause literally reads 'see which comes to you; choose one.'
segments.s193-g115: بفرمای is a reported-speech verb here (literary/polite perfective-ish form of فرمودن) functioning like 'he said/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that'. زین بر آن کت is a prepositional phrase: زین 'the saddle', بر 'on/upon', آن کت 'that coat'. هواست is a predicate: هوا (desire/wish) + ست (copula) = 'is desired / is the wish'. Together: 'He commanded that on that saddle that coat is the wish.'
segments.s193-g116: بسازند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive/jussive form of ساختن used here as an exhortation/command reported in indirect speech 'let them make/prepare'. اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کشته آید is a two-word clause: کشته (killed/slain) + آید (comes/happens) — an impersonal/nominalized passive idea 'if (a) killing occurs'. رواست is 'it is permitted/fit' (روا + ست), a judgmental copular conclusion. Grammatically this is a reported jussive + conditional clause giving permission contingent on a killing.
segments.s193-g117: یکی is an indefinite article-like 'one / a certain'. رخش is the noun 'steed/warhorse'. بودش is بود + enclitic ش giving 'it was his' or 'it was' with an enclosing referent; here it functions as the past copula 'there was'. بکردار is a compound adjective 'with the character/behavior of...' (ب‑ + کردار), used to ascribe a disposition; it links forward to گرگ which names that disposition: 'of wolf-like character.' Grammatically: Determiner + noun + copula + adjectival phrase 'of wolfly bearing.'
segments.s193-g118: A string of adjectives that modify the steed: کشیده here is a poetic adjective meaning 'long-drawn / gaunt / long of face', followed by زهار (an old poetic epithet), then و (and) بلند 'tall', و (and) سترگ 'big‑gazed' (سترگ describing the eye/sight). The adjectives are piled after the noun and linked by و; this is a typical Persian poetic adjective series describing appearance.
segments.s193-g119: ز بهر is a fixed poetic prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / for'. جهانجوی is a compound adjective or epithet 'world‑seeking / ambitious' modifying the following noun. مرد جوان is the noun phrase 'young man' (مرد 'man/hero' + جوان 'young'); word order is prepositional phrase + epithet + noun phrase.
segments.s193-g120: برو here is an imperative 'go' (continuing the sequence of commands/actions). برفگندند is a past tense 3rd‑person plural 'they threw/they cast' describing the act of flinging or placing. برگستوان is the locative/goal phrase (in older usage) indicating the part of the body receiving the object — literally rendered here as 'upon the arm/shoulder' (the lexical entry given is irregular in the list, but in context it functions as the phrase indicating where something was thrown). Together they form a short action clause 'they threw (it) onto the shoulder.'

sentences.s193-s65: He turned his face toward the white mountain, as did the warlike people.
sentences.s193-s37: And from there came a heart full of grief; with a head full of hatred I wandered off.
sentences.s193-s38: For from your horses a load of gloves — where does he stride so proudly and splendidly?
sentences.s193-s39: Give it so that I may don the war-weapon, until some brave man appears.
sentences.s193-s40: A certain Turk has gone onto the mountain's ridge; thus — look and go upon the troop.
sentences.s193-s5: Each one is braver than the other; like the shining sun, they are formed toward it.
sentences.s193-s6: But wisdom is not with the hero; a head without sense is like a body without spirit.
sentences.s193-s7: I fear there will be no victors in this (fight), unless Khosrow comes into the land of Turan.
sentences.s193-s8: Then we will all turn our backs, father—unless we can bring the enemies into our fists.
sentences.s193-s33: He said to him, “Did you not hear from the guide that in battle you must give thought to what is fitting?”
sentences.s193-s34: You have neither brains nor counsel or wisdom; whoever spoke thus was punished.
sentences.s193-s35: Bijan's heart, through his rashness, was stricken with pain; he swore an oath by the Lord, the Possessor.
sentences.s193-s36: He vowed that he would not remove this saddle from the horse's back unless he were slain by the vengeance of Zarasp.
sentences.s193-s1: He kept saying, and he fastened his cuirass tightly; the leather tore upon his body.
sentences.s193-s2: He rose from atop the grim dragon; striding proudly, he came along the road with the leather.
sentences.s193-s3: When Siyâvash descended and saw him, a cold wind rose from his heart.
sentences.s193-s4: He kept saying that this warlike host does not know the way of descent and ascent.
sentences.s193-s17: When he drew (it) on the bowstring and set a knot inside, your dagger could not find a passage through the armor.
sentences.s193-s18: Siyâvash dons his arms for battle; he does not fear arrows, shafts, or daggers.
sentences.s193-s19: He drew (and sent) the wheel and arrows toward the steed's flanks—unless the heavy beast grows weary.
sentences.s193-s20: He will dismount and return, unless, dragged like a spahbed, he is led with a shield about his neck.
sentences.s193-s21: He drew the bow and sent down a warlike shaft; then he turned that grip of the wheel toward his palm for his advantage.
sentences.s193-s22: He struck an arrow into Giv's horse's breast; the steed fell down, and from the blow it turned back anew.
sentences.s193-s23: From the roof of the white mountain a laugh arose; at that, Giv's wits/mind were diminished by the testimony.
sentences.s193-s24: All the squadrons advanced before Giv, saying, 'Thanks be to God, O commander (Niyu).'
sentences.s193-s25: That the horse is tired and you are not; it could not be tied again.
sentences.s193-s26: Bijan came up to Giv — a lion of a man — and spoke many words about the battle.
sentences.s193-s27: O Bab Shirâvazhan Tizchang, why did you not go to fight the elephant?
sentences.s193-s28: Why did he see behind you a rider who was at your hand for the battle?
sentences.s193-s13: He made many a little child fatherless; he consigned many to the mountains, rivers, and wilderness.
sentences.s193-s14: The father, too, because of him became bereft of sons; he once again laid his neck before the male lion.
sentences.s193-s15: At Bayran he slew your brother; he crossed the Jeyhun and saw no ship.
sentences.s193-s16: They call him Vra-giw Pilist — that's enough — for in battle the sea is Nile-blue, and that's enough.
sentences.s193-s61: Giv's heart was filled with smoke at those words; when he pondered the wide descent...
sentences.s193-s62: He sent and had Gostaham summoned to me, and (he) had many fine tales read (recited).
sentences.s193-s63: He sent Siavash's armor to him — and likewise one of the royal helmets of his.
sentences.s193-s64: Gostaham brought the battle-armor; Bijan put it on and became renowned for his deeds.
sentences.s193-s41: He answered thus: “This is not the time to show your face; do not stare at the clouds, and do not go seeking calamities.”
sentences.s193-s42: Zarasp the sepahdar, like Rioniz the spahbed, who has nothing in the world.
sentences.s193-s43: Your father — he who smashed living elephants — even the turning heavens do not look upon the wanderer.
sentences.s193-s44: They turned away from him, hearts full of pain; no one brought aid, nor could the flinty rock be moved.
sentences.s193-s49: He said to him, “Then I have no wide road; my own reason is not aware of this swiftness.”
sentences.s193-s50: The world is full of ups and downs, and the plain — if one must pass from here in such a way...
sentences.s193-s51: Take my burden — now the cuirass tugs; two will remain if one of these saddles pulls.
sentences.s193-s52: I cannot find another equal to him in color, swiftness, strength and loftiness.
sentences.s193-s9: Tell me who that proud rider is — for one must weep at his hand and his sword.
sentences.s193-s10: He looked from the high summit; a lowly wretch — and on the meadow a thorn grew.
sentences.s193-s11: He said to him, “That grim dragon of hatred — which plucks birds from the air, (bringing) my blood/ruin.”
sentences.s193-s12: For the hand (power) of your ancestor bound the elders; two armies of the Turks were shattered together.
sentences.s193-s53: Bijan said to him, 'Bakin, Zarasp — I will go on foot; I do not want the horse myself.'
sentences.s193-s54: He replied thus: 'I would not take even a single hair from you, neither more nor less.'
sentences.s193-s55: If I had ten thousand leaves, each edge full of regal gems,
sentences.s193-s56: I would not withhold that from you — neither treasure, nor life, nor steed, nor sword.
sentences.s193-s29: From the Turk such a tired steed fell into (his) hands; he rushed off, flustered, to bring it to the drunk.
sentences.s193-s30: He said to him, 'When Bargi was killed, I gave him the head at once.'
sentences.s193-s31: He kept uttering coarse words; when Bijan saw this, he showed/turned his back.
sentences.s193-s32: Giv, angered, and because of the boastful/loose words, once struck him on the head with a whip.
sentences.s193-s45: Unless a vulture's feather served as his guide, he would be hurrying toward that fortress which lies in ambush.
sentences.s193-s46: Bijan said to him, 'Do not break my heart; now let me tear the mane and the arm apart.'
sentences.s193-s47: Once I swore a solemn oath by the moon: by the just/ruling power of the world and the country‑lord king.
sentences.s193-s48: From this Turk I will not recover my steed; my time will (come to pass) unless it be like Zarrasp.
sentences.s193-s57: Go and take a look at the baragīhs; whichever one suits you, choose it.
sentences.s193-s58: He said, 'Arrange that saddle with that coat as you desire; if anyone is slain, it is permitted.'
sentences.s193-s59: One of his steeds was, in character, like a wolf — long‑drawn‑faced, tall, and with a wide/gazing eye.
sentences.s193-s60: For the world‑seeking young man they flung (it) upon his shoulder.
